text: I do not agree, but I will support the decision.

When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team.
The last three sprints closed two days late.
What is getting in the way?
I need you to flag the risk on day three.
Marina, can I give you some feedback? The last three sprints closed two days late.
The tickets came late. That is not my fault.
I am not talking about fault. I am talking about the behavior, and I want to be specific: sprint twelve, thirteen and fourteen.
So now I am the problem of the project?
No. Your code is strong. The migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team.
Nobody told me it was a problem.
That is on me, and I am sorry. You should be aware of it. So, what is getting in the way?
The reviews. I wait three days for a code review, every sprint.
That I can fix. I need you to flag it on day three, and I will adjust the review queue. Deal?
Can I give you some feedback?

Diogo, I read your sprint report. Three sprints late, and the reason is always the same person. You have not spoken to her, have you? I know it is uncomfortable. But if you do not say it, two things happen. She never gets better, because nobody told her. And the team sees that late is acceptable. Do not be harsh, and do not make a speech. Be specific: what happened, how many days, what it costs the test team. Then ask her one question, and be quiet. Ninety percent of the time the answer surprises you. And Diogo, do not send an email. Say it to her face.

Marina, thank you for the time. Can I give you some feedback? It is not easy, and I want to be specific. The last three sprints closed two days late. I am talking about the deadline, not about the quality of your code -- the migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is real: the test team loses two days, every time, and they work the weekend. So my question is simple. What is getting in the way? Please be honest with me. I am not here to blame you. I am here because I need you to flag the risk on day three, not on the last day, so we can adjust the plan together. If something is blocking you, tell me and I will remove it.

Diogo opens the conversation and asks if he can give feedback. He describes the behavior: the last three sprints closed two days late. Marina gets defensive, and says the tickets came late. Diogo names the impact on the test team, and asks what is getting in the way. Marina says she waits three days for a code review, and Diogo asks her to flag it on day three.

Honest is good. Harsh is not.
